dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for breathing problems at the residence on Peach Street, [CITY].
Hello, I'm Emily, an EMT. What brought you to call 911 today?
I have chest pain, shortness of breath, and I've been dizzy and sweating.
Mr. Thomas, can you open your eyes for me?
Can you tell me your name and where we are?
Mr. Thomas, can you squeeze my fingers with each hand?
Your airway is clear, no signs of obstruction. Let's keep you in this upright position for now.
Your breathing is fast and shallow. Air moves in both lungs, and breath sounds are equal bilaterally.
Mr. Thomas, we are going to check your blood oxygen level. It is currently at 89% on room air.
Your pulse is strong and rapid. Skin is warm and diaphoretic. No cyanosis present.
His pulse is 112 beats per minute.
Respirations are 28 per minute and shallow in depth.
Blood pressure is 130 over 79 mmHg, right arm.
Mr. Thomas's blood sugar was measured at the initial assessment, 147 mg/dL.
Can you tell me any other symptoms besides dizziness, sweating, and chest pain?
I'm also short of breath and cannot seem to catch a full breath.
You have a noted allergy to penicillin. Have you had any reactions?
Only a mild one years ago. I avoid it now.
Can you list all your regular medications?
Chlorthalidone, meloxicam, gabapentin, Prazosin, aspirin, metformin, lovastatin, bupropion, and Albuterol.
You mentioned a heart attack in the past. When was that?
It was about ten years ago.
When was the last time you had food or drink?
I can't remember exactly.
When did these symptoms start today?
I woke up at around 2:00 PM feeling dizzy and very sweaty, and it hasn't stopped since.
When did your chest pain begin?
Same time as the dizziness-around 2 PM.
Is there anything that makes the chest pain better or worse?
Nothing changes it. It remains sharp and constant.
How would you describe the type of chest pain you are feeling?
It's like a knife cutting through the center of my chest.
Please indicate where the chest pain is located.
(Points to center of chest.) It's right here.
How severe is the chest pain on a scale of 0 to 10?
It's a 10. It doesn't let up.
Has the severity of pain changed over time?
No, it has been constant.
Your skin is warm, moist from perspiration, and normal in color with no visible bruising or scars.
Mr. Thomas, what is the date today, and where are you?
I'm Jim Thomas, and I believe it's February 18. I'm at home because I don't feel good.
There is no drainage, blockage, or redness in your ears, nose, or throat.
We are now placing a 4-lead ECG to monitor your heart rhythm continuously.
The EKG shows a rhythm of sinus tachycardia at this time.
We are going to insert an IV to administer fluids, to ensure your circulatory support.
The IV has been placed in your left AC vein. We are now giving a slow saline flush to confirm patency.
We are starting 250 mL of normal saline via IV to help increase blood pressure.
A 12-lead EKG is now being performed for a more detailed cardiac evaluation.
The 12-lead EKG has been completed and shows a normal sinus rhythm with signs of LVH and nonspecific T wave changes.
The EKG also shows moderate right axis deviation. No acute cardiac changes are observed at this time.
I am placing a nasal cannula ETCO₂ monitor to ensure continuous breathing assessment.
Mr. Thomas, your oxygen saturation is 96% on 2 liters per minute via nasal cannula.
Based on symptoms, prior heart attack, and EKG, we are treating this under the chest pain cardiac suspected protocol.
Updated vitals: pulse 95, respirations 25, and blood pressure 86 over 54.
IV fluids are stable and the IV site is clean, no swelling or painful injection site noted.
The rhythm has transitioned to normal sinus rhythm.
We conducted another 12-lead EKG and confirmed that all findings remain unchanged.
Mr. Thomas's oxygen saturation is now 100% on room air.
Pulse is 109, respirations 23, and blood pressure is 103 over 69.
Mr. Thomas will be transported to the [COMPANY_NAME] for further cardiology assessment and monitoring.
Mr. Thomas is now secured onto the stretcher using a 5-point harness.
The transport vehicle is prepared and will arrive for immediate transfer to the [COMPANY_NAME] emergency department.
Upon arrival, Mr. Thomas will be taken directly to trauma bay 7 for immediate processing.
We will give a full report of symptoms, interventions, and vital signs to the hospital team upon arrival.
Have you recently used any recreational drugs, and if so, when?
I used cocaine about two days ago. I'm unsure if I used it again today.
Mr. Thomas is alert and oriented with a Glasgow Coma Scale of 15. He remains diaphoretic.
A 12-lead EKG was obtained, showing minimal left ventricular hypertrophy and nonspecific T wave changes.
Mr. Thomas's stroke scale came back negative, and no other neurological findings were observed.
Mr. Thomas's blood glucose is currently measuring at 0 following our second assessment.
Prior to transport, an 18-gauge IV catheter was inserted in the left AC vein.
Full documentation of all vital signs, rhythms, and medications has been compiled for handover at the trauma bay.
The nasal cannula ETCO₂ monitor has been functioning with consistent square waveform readings during transport.
Delayed handover was necessary due to patient priority and pending critical care preparations at the receiving facility.
All procedures were conducted using provided protocols for suspected acute cardiac chest pain.
